name: deserialize_external_body_returns_any_marshalable
reason: All `external_body` `deserialize` implementations (usize, Vec<u8>, Option<T>, Vec<T>, (T,U)) have trivially satisfiable `None => true` ensures — they could always return `None` and vacuously satisfy the spec, making the deserialize contract useless for proving roundtrip properties

name: option_none_serialize_tag_zero
reason: `None` serializes to a single byte `[0]` while `Some(x)` uses `[1] + x.ghost_serialize()` — but since `ghost_serialize` at the trait level is `external_body` with only a `recommends`, there's no guarantee that the open spec definitions here actually match what the external_body trait-level function returns

name: tuple_serialize_ambiguous_no_delimiter
code:
proof fn phi_3_tuple_serialize_ambiguous_no_delimiter(a1: Vec<u8>, a2: Vec<u8>, b1: Vec<u8>, b2: Vec<u8>)
    requires
        a1.is_marshalable(),
        a2.is_marshalable(),
        b1.is_marshalable(),
        b2.is_marshalable(),
        a1@ == seq![1u8, 2u8],
        a2@ == seq![3u8, 4u8, 5u8],
        b1@ == seq![1u8, 2u8, 3u8],
        b2@ == seq![4u8, 5u8],
    ensures
        (a1, a2).ghost_serialize() == (b1, b2).ghost_serialize(),
{
}
reason: Tuple serialization concatenates the two components without any length delimiter or separator — two different tuples with different split points can produce identical serializations, breaking injectivity

name: vec_u8_deserialize_always_none_valid
reason: The `external_body` `deserialize` for `Vec<u8>` can always return `None` since the ensures clause has `None => true` — there is no liveness/completeness guarantee that deserialization succeeds when given valid serialized data, so the spec is too weak to prove any roundtrip property

name: usize_deserialize_external_body_unconstrained
reason: `usize::deserialize` is `external_body` with no custom ensures — it inherits the trait ensures which only says the result `is_marshalable()` (i.e., `<= u64::MAX`) if `Some`, but the implementation is completely unverified and could return any usize including values that truncated from the u64 in the byte stream
